test_rationale: This test uses a minimal authoritative zone with SOA and NS at the apex plus one helper TXT RRset, then sends a single delete_name for the apex itself. That is the clearest RFC 2136 choice point because the operation nominally deletes all RRsets at the name, yet RFC 2181 requires the apex to retain SOA and NS. The follow-up SOA and NS queries make any rejection-vs-acceptance difference visible in both the UPDATE reply and the resulting apex contents.

group_1.behavior: Rejects delete_name at the zone apex as invalid or refuses to apply it, retaining the mandatory apex RRsets.
group_1.post_state: campus.edu. still has SOA and NS, and the helper TXT may also remain unchanged.

group_2.behavior: Accepts the UPDATE and removes non-mandatory apex data, with mandatory apex content handled differently or repaired later.
group_2.post_state: campus.edu. may lose the TXT helper and possibly diverge in whether SOA/NS are immediately observable.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 §3.4.4 defines delete_name as deleting all RRsets at a name, but RFC 2181 §6.1 and §7 require SOA and NS at the zone apex and do not specify how dynamic update should map a delete_name targeting the apex to an RCODE or mandatory-record handling. Implementations may therefore disagree on whether to refuse the UPDATE or to apply it and preserve/repair the apex contents differently.
